steps.title: Identify what determines the conclusion
steps.content:
Meng's conclusion rests on a specific ratio: payload capacity = holding force ÷ weight (higher ratio = greater capacity).

The passage establishes that Ultra-Fast has 4× Yale Model T's holding force (56 newtons vs. roughly 14 newtons).

To support the conclusion that Ultra-Fast has higher capacity, the graph must show that this 4× holding-force advantage isn't canceled by a proportionally larger weight.

steps.title: Match the graph data to the ratio calculation
steps.content:
Choice D shows Ultra-Fast weighs only slightly more than Yale (~505 vs. ~490 grams).

With 4× the holding force but nearly equal weight, Ultra-Fast's holding force-to-weight ratio is far greater, directly supporting the conclusion.

Choices A, B, and C either don't compare these two robots' relative capacity or introduce comparisons irrelevant to the Ultra-Fast–Yale holding force ratio. That matches choice D — D is correct.
